shapes.name: subtitle.0
shapes.text: AP Calculus BC Unit 6: Integration and Accumulation of Change

shapes.name: bullets.0
shapes.text:
• Unit 6 turns rates of change into total change.
• The game questions are built from this lesson sequence.
• The project is a Notion website plus a live JavaScript quiz embed.

shapes.name: notes.0
shapes.text: Open by showing the Notion page, then explain that this slide deck teaches the same concepts tested in the multiplayer quiz.

shapes.name: footerLeft.0
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: Open by showing the Notion page, then explain that this slide deck teaches the same concepts tested in the multiplayer quiz.

shapes.name: bullets.1
shapes.text:
• Estimate accumulation with rectangles and trapezoids.
• Represent exact accumulation with definite integrals.
• Use the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus to connect derivatives and integrals.
• Choose antiderivative techniques when integrals are not basic.

shapes.name: notes.1
shapes.text: This mirrors College Board's Unit 6 outline: definite integrals, Riemann sums, accumulation functions, FTC, antiderivatives, techniques, and improper integrals.

shapes.name: footerLeft.1
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: This mirrors College Board's Unit 6 outline: definite integrals, Riemann sums, accumulation functions, FTC, antiderivatives, techniques, and improper integrals.

shapes.name: subtitle.2
shapes.text: Differentiation asks for an instant; integration asks for the total.

shapes.name: formula.2
shapes.text: If v(t) is velocity, then ∫ v(t)dt is displacement.

shapes.name: bullets.2
shapes.text:
• A derivative tells how fast something is changing.
• An integral gathers those tiny changes over an interval.
• Positive area adds; negative area subtracts.
• Units multiply: rate units times input units.

shapes.name: notes.2
shapes.text: Use a motion example: miles/hour times hours gives miles. This makes the integral feel less abstract.

shapes.name: footerLeft.2
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: Use a motion example: miles/hour times hours gives miles. This makes the integral feel less abstract.

shapes.name: subtitle.3
shapes.text: When a rate is known, the integral gives the total change.

shapes.name: bullets.3
shapes.text:
• The function inside the integral is usually a rate.
• The interval tells when the accumulation starts and stops.
• The answer is not a rate anymore; it is a total amount.
• Always check units in word problems.

shapes.name: example.3
shapes.text:
Example
If r(t)=3t^2 inches/hour, then ∫_0^2 3t^2dt=8 inches.

shapes.name: notes.3
shapes.text: Connect this to the Frozen Accumulation Vault question in the game.

shapes.name: footerLeft.3
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: Connect this to the Frozen Accumulation Vault question in the game.

shapes.name: subtitle.4
shapes.text: Rectangles estimate area before exact integrals are available.

shapes.name: bullets.4
shapes.text:
• Left sums use the left endpoint of each subinterval.
• Right sums use the right endpoint.
• Midpoint sums use the center of each subinterval.
• For increasing functions, left sums underestimate and right sums overestimate.

shapes.name: example.4
shapes.text:
Example
Left sum on [0,3] with Δx=1 uses f(0)+f(1)+f(2).

shapes.name: notes.4
shapes.text: Have students identify which x-values are used before doing arithmetic.

shapes.name: footerLeft.4
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: Have students identify which x-values are used before doing arithmetic.

shapes.name: bullets.5
shapes.text:
• The first and last y-values are counted once.
• Interior y-values are counted twice.
• Trapezoids usually improve on rectangles for smooth functions.
• Concavity helps decide overestimate or underestimate.

shapes.name: example.5
shapes.text:
Example
For y-values 1,4,7 with Δx=2: T=(2/2)(1+2·4+7)=16.

shapes.name: notes.5
shapes.text: Emphasize the pattern: single, double, single.

shapes.name: footerLeft.5
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: Emphasize the pattern: single, double, single.

shapes.name: subtitle.6
shapes.text: A definite integral is the limit of Riemann sums.

shapes.name: bullets.6
shapes.text:
• The summation expression estimates area.
• The limit makes the rectangles infinitely thin.
• The integral symbol represents exact signed accumulation.
• The lower and upper bounds come from the interval.

shapes.name: notes.6
shapes.text: This is a key bridge slide. Students often miss that the interval becomes the bounds.

shapes.name: footerLeft.6
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: This is a key bridge slide. Students often miss that the interval becomes the bounds.

shapes.name: subtitle.7
shapes.text: Most simplification comes from interval logic.

shapes.name: bullets.7
shapes.text:
• Reversing bounds changes the sign.
• Adding adjacent intervals combines accumulated change.
• Constants can be factored out.
• The integral from a to a is zero.

shapes.name: example.7
shapes.text:
Example
If ∫_1^4 f=9 and ∫_1^2 f=3, then ∫_2^4 f=6.

shapes.name: notes.7
shapes.text: This slide matches several fast game questions where students can score by knowing properties.

shapes.name: footerLeft.7
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: This slide matches several fast game questions where students can score by knowing properties.

shapes.name: subtitle.8
shapes.text: The derivative of an accumulation function gives the original integrand.

shapes.name: bullets.8
shapes.text:
• The variable upper bound is the key signal.
• Replace t with x after differentiating.
• This does not require finding an antiderivative first.
• It connects area accumulation to instantaneous rate.

shapes.name: notes.8
shapes.text: Say: the integral accumulates, the derivative asks how fast the accumulation is changing.

shapes.name: footerLeft.8
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: Say: the integral accumulates, the derivative asks how fast the accumulation is changing.

shapes.name: subtitle.9
shapes.text: A non-x upper bound adds an extra derivative.

shapes.name: bullets.9
shapes.text:
• First plug the upper bound into the integrand.
• Then multiply by the derivative of the upper bound.
• If the variable is on the lower bound, include a negative sign.
• This is a common AP-style trap.

shapes.name: notes.9
shapes.text: Highlight the two moves: substitute, multiply.

shapes.name: footerLeft.9
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: Highlight the two moves: substitute, multiply.

shapes.name: subtitle.10
shapes.text: Definite integrals can be evaluated with antiderivatives.

shapes.name: bullets.10
shapes.text:
• Find any antiderivative F of f.
• Evaluate at the upper bound.
• Subtract the value at the lower bound.
• Do not add +C in a definite integral final answer.

shapes.name: notes.10
shapes.text: This is the most-used exact evaluation tool in the unit.

shapes.name: footerLeft.10
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: This is the most-used exact evaluation tool in the unit.

shapes.name: subtitle.11
shapes.text: Increase the exponent, then divide by the new exponent.

shapes.name: bullets.11
shapes.text:
• The exponent n=-1 is the logarithm exception.
• Constants multiply through.
• Indefinite integrals need +C.
• Check your answer by differentiating.

shapes.name: notes.11
shapes.text: Ask students why n=-1 cannot use the same formula.

shapes.name: footerLeft.11
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: Ask students why n=-1 cannot use the same formula.

shapes.name: bullets.12
shapes.text:
• Exponential functions often stay the same.
• Reciprocal linear patterns often produce logarithms.
• Trig integrals require sign discipline.
• Inverse trig forms appear after completing the square.

shapes.name: notes.12
shapes.text: This slide is the formula checkpoint before substitution.

shapes.name: footerLeft.12
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: This slide is the formula checkpoint before substitution.

shapes.name: subtitle.13
shapes.text: Use substitution when a derivative is hiding inside the integrand.

shapes.name: bullets.13
shapes.text:
• Look for an inside function and its derivative.
• Rewrite the integral fully in terms of u.
• Integrate with respect to u.
• Substitute x back unless the problem is definite and you changed bounds.

shapes.name: notes.13
shapes.text: The phrase 'derivative is already there' is the student-friendly test.

shapes.name: footerLeft.13
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: The phrase 'derivative is already there' is the student-friendly test.

shapes.name: subtitle.14
shapes.text: Change the bounds or substitute back before evaluating.

shapes.name: bullets.14
shapes.text:
• Changing bounds keeps everything in u.
• Substituting back means you keep original x-bounds.
• Do not mix u-bounds with x-expressions.
• This is one of the most common notation mistakes.

shapes.name: notes.14
shapes.text: Make students choose one path and stick to it.

shapes.name: footerLeft.14
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: Make students choose one path and stick to it.

shapes.name: subtitle.15
shapes.text: The derivative of the denominator is the giveaway.

shapes.name: bullets.15
shapes.text:
• Absolute value matters for real logarithms.
• A constant multiple may need adjustment.
• Linear denominators are common entry-level examples.
• More complex rational functions may need partial fractions first.

shapes.name: notes.15
shapes.text: Distinguish between plain log patterns and partial fraction patterns.

shapes.name: footerLeft.15
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: Distinguish between plain log patterns and partial fraction patterns.

shapes.name: subtitle.16
shapes.text: Quadratics can become inverse tangent forms.

shapes.name: bullets.16
shapes.text:
• Complete the square in the denominator.
• Shift x into a new expression like x+2.
• Match the denominator to u^2+a^2.
• The coefficient is 1/a, not a.

shapes.name: notes.16
shapes.text: This is a BC extension-style technique that rewards algebra patience.

shapes.name: footerLeft.16
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: This is a BC extension-style technique that rewards algebra patience.

shapes.name: subtitle.17
shapes.text: Algebra can turn a scary integral into a basic one.

shapes.name: bullets.17
shapes.text:
• Divide each term by the denominator when possible.
• Use long division when the numerator degree is larger.
• Simplifying first reduces technique overload.
• After simplification, use the basic toolbox.

shapes.name: notes.17
shapes.text: Say: do algebra before calculus when algebra makes the function simpler.

shapes.name: footerLeft.17
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: Say: do algebra before calculus when algebra makes the function simpler.

shapes.name: subtitle.18
shapes.text: Use parts for products where substitution does not cleanly work.

shapes.name: bullets.18
shapes.text:
• Choose u to become simpler when differentiated.
• Choose dv to be easy to integrate.
• Products with x and trig/exponential functions are classic.
• The second integral should be easier than the first.

shapes.name: notes.18
shapes.text: Use LIATE as a heuristic, not a law.

shapes.name: footerLeft.18
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: Use LIATE as a heuristic, not a law.

shapes.name: subtitle.19
shapes.text: Split rational functions after factoring the denominator.

shapes.name: bullets.19
shapes.text:
• Factor the denominator first.
• Create one fraction term per linear factor.
• Solve for the constants.
• Integrate the separated terms, usually into logarithms.

shapes.name: notes.19
shapes.text: This is another BC extension-style method and appears in the game.

shapes.name: footerLeft.19
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: This is another BC extension-style method and appears in the game.

shapes.name: subtitle.20
shapes.text: Use limits for infinity or vertical asymptotes.

shapes.name: bullets.20
shapes.text:
• Infinite intervals require a limit.
• Vertical asymptotes inside or at endpoints require a limit.
• Convergent means the limit is finite.
• Divergent means the limit is infinite or does not exist.

shapes.name: notes.20
shapes.text: Make students name where the problem is improper before solving.

shapes.name: footerLeft.20
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: Make students name where the problem is improper before solving.

shapes.name: bullets.21
shapes.text:
• p>1 converges on [1,∞).
• p≤1 diverges on [1,∞).
• Near 0, ∫_0^1 1/x^p dx converges if p<1.
• Always check the interval before using a shortcut.

shapes.name: notes.21
shapes.text: Compare the infinity case with the zero-endpoint case.

shapes.name: footerLeft.21
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: Compare the infinity case with the zero-endpoint case.

shapes.name: subtitle.22
shapes.text: The hardest part is often choosing the first move.

shapes.name: formula.22
shapes.text: Simplify → basic rule → u-sub → parts → partial fractions → improper limit

shapes.name: bullets.22
shapes.text:
• Simplify algebraically before choosing a technique.
• Check for an inside function and derivative.
• For products, consider integration by parts.
• For rational functions, factor and consider division or partial fractions.

shapes.name: notes.22
shapes.text: This is the decision tree students should use during the game.

shapes.name: footerLeft.22
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: This is the decision tree students should use during the game.

shapes.name: subtitle.23
shapes.text: Most misses are notation or sign errors, not concept failures.

shapes.name: formula.23
shapes.text: Differentiate your antiderivative to check it.

shapes.name: bullets.23
shapes.text:
• Forgetting +C on indefinite integrals.
• Adding +C to final definite-integral answers.
• Mixing changed u-bounds with x-expressions.
• Dropping a negative sign when reversing bounds.
• Forgetting absolute value in logarithmic antiderivatives.

shapes.name: notes.23
shapes.text: Use this before starting the live quiz so students know what to watch for.

shapes.name: footerLeft.23
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: Use this before starting the live quiz so students know what to watch for.

shapes.name: subtitle.24
shapes.text: Correct reasoning matters, but speed and streaks add game energy.

shapes.name: formula.24
shapes.text: Score = base + speed bonus + streak bonus + badge bonus

shapes.name: bullets.24
shapes.text:
• Each question has a base point value.
• Faster correct answers earn a speed bonus.
• Consecutive correct answers build a streak bonus.
• Three-question streaks earn a themed badge bonus.

shapes.name: notes.24
shapes.text: This is where you explain that the code creates classroom engagement, not just a worksheet.

shapes.name: footerLeft.24
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: This is where you explain that the code creates classroom engagement, not just a worksheet.

shapes.name: subtitle.25
shapes.text: Use these before launching the multiplayer game.

shapes.name: bullets.25
shapes.text:
• Question 1: Which method fits ∫cos(x)e^{sin x}dx?
• Question 2: Evaluate ∫_0^2 6t^2dt.
• Question 3: Does ∫_1^∞1/x^{3/2}dx converge?
• Question 4: Find d/dx ∫_1^{x^2}cos(t)dt.

shapes.name: notes.25
shapes.text: Have students answer verbally first, then start the game.

shapes.name: footerLeft.25
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: Have students answer verbally first, then start the game.

shapes.name: subtitle.26
shapes.text: Notion is the lesson hub; JavaScript powers the live game.

shapes.name: bullets.26
shapes.text:
• The Notion gallery acts like the project navigation.
• The live quiz runs as a real website at the hosted URL.
• Notion embeds that website so classmates can play inside the page.
• The question bank remains editable in data/questions.js.

shapes.name: notes.26
shapes.text: This is the clean explanation for why the project uses both Notion and JavaScript.

shapes.name: footerLeft.26
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: This is the clean explanation for why the project uses both Notion and JavaScript.

shapes.name: subtitle.27
shapes.text: The game produces engagement; the notebook proves learning.

shapes.name: bullets.27
shapes.text:
• Students record at least four solved questions.
• Each record includes the integration method.
• Each record includes the final answer.
• Each record includes one sentence explaining why the method works.

shapes.name: notes.27
shapes.text: This makes the project teacher-friendly because it includes an assessment product.

shapes.name: footerLeft.27
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: This makes the project teacher-friendly because it includes an assessment product.

shapes.name: subtitle.28
shapes.text: A concise way to explain the project to your teacher.

shapes.name: bullets.28
shapes.text:
• I built a Disney-themed Notion website for AP Calculus BC Unit 6.
• The website includes a custom multiplayer JavaScript quiz game.
• The game uses live room codes, timed questions, points, streaks, and badges.
• The questions and lesson slides can be edited for future classes.

shapes.name: notes.28
shapes.text: End by showing data/questions.js and explaining that the project is reusable.

shapes.name: footerLeft.28
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC

notes.text: End by showing data/questions.js and explaining that the project is reusable.

shapes.name: subtitle.29
shapes.text: Built around AP Calculus BC Unit 6.

shapes.name: formula.29
shapes.text: College Board Unit 6 = Integration and Accumulation of Change

shapes.name: bullets.29
shapes.text:
• College Board describes Unit 6 as integration and accumulation of change.
• The unit includes definite integrals, Riemann sums, accumulation functions, FTC, antiderivatives, techniques, and improper integrals.
• College Board lists Unit 6 as 17%-20% of the AP Calculus BC multiple-choice section.
• This project is an unofficial classroom theme, not affiliated with Disney or College Board.

shapes.name: notes.29
shapes.text: Source checked April 30, 2026: College Board AP Calculus BC course page and AP Central course overview.

shapes.name: footerLeft.29
shapes.text: Integral Imperial Intelligence • Unit 6 Calc BC
